title: First-Time Homebuyer: Down Payment vs Financial Security
goals: Avoid PMI or minimize PMI cost
Maintain adequate emergency fund after purchase
Evaluate whether to buy now or wait 18-24 months
constraints: Active car loan increases DTI to 44%
Closing costs could deplete emergency fund
key_risks: Emergency fund depletion at time of maximum financial exposure (new homeowner)
DTI at 44% approaching lender maximum with car loan

planning_issues.description: 11% down triggers PMI ($1,600-3,200/year); 20% down requires $76,000; emergency fund at risk from closing costs
Car loan ($430/month) pushes DTI to 44%; paying it off drops DTI to 38% and improves mortgage qualification
PITI of $2,799/month = 38.2% of gross income — above the 28% benchmark; housing cost ratio at boundary
Rate environment: 7.0% mortgage adds $762/month vs 3.5% rate; first-time buyer programs may offer below-market rates

key_recommendations: Investigate Colorado CHFA and first-time buyer programs — may change affordability math
Wait 6 more months: save to 15% down ($57,000) minimum
Maintain $15,000 emergency fund at closing — do not let closing costs drain below 3 months
Pay off car loan aggressively before applying for mortgage
Consider rate buydown: 1-point buydown (~$3,380) reduces rate to 6.0%; break-even 18-24 months
follow_up_questions: Is Miguel eligible for Colorado CHFA (income and purchase price limits)?
Does employer offer home purchase assistance benefits?
What is Miguel's credit score? (720+ qualifies for best conventional rates)
Is there flexibility on purchase price? ($340-350K meaningfully changes DTI)
Has a 2-1 rate buydown strategy been evaluated?
outcome_summary: Miguel waited 8 months, saved $20,000 more, paid off car loan, purchased at $365,000 with 17% down. Housing cost ratio 33%.
key_lesson: First-time buyers must model the full cost of PMI, DTI impact of existing debt, and emergency fund depletion risk. A 6-12 month wait to reach a stronger financial position often dominates the cost of continued renting.
